data.title: First Merchants expects mid-single-digit 2026 loan growth while targeting $111M-$114M quarterly expenses after First Savings integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Merchants Corporation (FRME) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We now operate 127 banking centers, reflecting the addition of Southern Indiana following the First Savings acquisition." (CEO & Director Mark Hardwick)

  • "First quarter reported net income was $27.7 million or $0.45 per diluted share." (CEO & Director Hardwick) "Excluding these items, adjusted earnings per share totaled $1.03." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

  • "We strategically repositioned $357 million of mortgage loans from held for investment to held for sale, and we expect to complete the sale of these loans by the end of the second quarter." (CEO & Director Hardwick) "This repositioning resulted in a $29.8 million mark-to-market charge in the quarter." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

  • "These loans carry a weighted average coupon of 3.46% and the liquidity provided by their sale will be used to immediately pay down higher cost deposits and over time, will be deployed into commercial loans at a 6% plus yield." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

  • "While this quarter's organic growth was flat, I remain confident in our expected mid-single-digit loan growth through the course of 2026." (President Michael Stewart)

  • "There was meaningful growth in total revenues in Q1. Net interest income grew $12.2 million and noninterest income grew $2.5 million linked quarter." (Executive VP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Michele Kawiecki)

  • "Actual tangible book value dilution was only 2.4% versus 4.8% that we shared at announcement, and the tangible book value earn-back is now estimated to be 2.4 years." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

  • "Our overall asset quality remains stable." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer John Martin) "We are not seeing broad-based deterioration across the portfolio." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Martin)

Outlook

  • "I do feel confident with that mid-single-digit growth rate and reaffirm." (President Stewart)

  • "I would expect there to be a few basis points of pickup on margin through the year." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki) "Deposit rates... will be relatively steady." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

  • "The quarterly expense total like on a quarterly run rate will probably be somewhere between $111 million to $114 million." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki) "The cost synergies we expect to gain from the First Savings acquisition are on track." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

  • "I would expect Q1 to see a lift of about 3% to 4% in the coming quarters." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

Financial results

  • "Adjusted performance metrics remain strong, including an adjusted ROA of 1.25% and an adjusted return on tangible common equity exceeding 14%." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

  • "Total assets stand at $21.1 billion with $15.3 billion in loans and $16.5 billion in deposits." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

  • "Net interest income on a fully tax equivalent basis of $157.7 million increased $12.4 million linked quarter." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki) "Our quarterly net interest margin of 3.35% increased 6 basis points from prior quarter." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

  • "Noninterest expense for the quarter totaled $125.1 million and included $17 million in acquisition-related costs." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

  • "This quarter, we had net charge-offs of $10.3 million and recorded a $4.9 million provision." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki) "The allowance for credit losses totaled $212.5 million... representing a coverage ratio of 1.39%." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

  • "We remain well capitalized with the common equity tier 1 ratio at 11.22%." (Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: "if you're still comfortable with -- I think we talked about 6% to 8% growth for the year last quarter"; President Stewart: "I do feel confident with that mid-single-digit growth rate and reaffirm."

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: "where you see [margin] moving going forward"; Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki: "it will be a little more neutral right out of the gate" on the loan sale, and "we will see margin pick up a few basis points."

  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: "deposit migration into noninterest-bearing" and whether deposit costs can move lower; Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki: "it's been very stable" and "I don't anticipate that we'll be lowering deposit rates meaningfully through the year."

  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Basel III proposal and buybacks; Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki: "our estimate is that it will benefit us probably somewhere between like 50 to 80 basis points" and "we will continue to be active in the buyback space."

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: "any other need for additional balance sheet optimization"; CEO & Director Hardwick: "we're not anticipating anything else" beyond the repositioning.

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: specialty verticals; President Stewart: triple-net lease production "remained very stable," first-lien HELOC is "a complete originate and sell," and "they were doing around $100 million of SBA transactions last year."

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: expense outlook; Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki: "the quarterly expense total... will probably be somewhere between $111 million to $114 million."

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: fee income and vertical-driven gain-on-sale; Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki: "normalized level of total noninterest income... was $35.6 million" and "I would expect Q1 to see a lift of about 3% to 4% in the coming quarters."

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: charge-offs and First Savings; Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Martin: "The charge-offs for the first quarter were really legacy First Merchants" and "it wasn't really driven at all" by First Savings.

  • Brian Martin, Brean: repricing and tax rate; Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki: "we've got about $100 million that matures at a rate of about 4.5% each quarter" and "13% effective tax rate is what we would expect."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly skeptical, pressing for visibility on loan growth durability, margin mechanics after the loan sale, deposit cost flexibility, and capital actions (e.g., "how you're thinking about the impact" and "any other need for additional balance sheet optimization").

  • Management sentiment was positive in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with repeated confidence language including "remain confident," "on track," and "we're not anticipating anything else," alongside CEO & Director Hardwick’s closing view: "we remain incredibly optimistic about the remainder of the year."

  • Versus the prior quarter, management continued an optimistic tone but shifted from planning to reporting: Q4 discussed evaluating "some type of balance sheet kind of repositioning," while Q1 framed it as already executed (the $357 million transfer) and "not anticipating anything else." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The acquisition moved from imminent to operational: Q4 said "we will actually close this weekend on February 1, 2026," while Q1 reported "2 months of operating results from First Savings" and set "May 15" as the integration milestone. (CEO & Director Hardwick; Executive VP, CFO Kawiecki)

  • Balance sheet optimization narrowed from evaluation to a defined action: Q4 described reviewing "some of our lowest yielding bonds as well as some of our lowest yielding loans," while Q1 highlighted a single, sized mortgage repositioning and said, "we're not anticipating anything else." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

  • Guidance language stayed consistent on loan growth, with Q4 describing planning for "6%, 7%, 8%" and Q1 reaffirming "mid-single-digit" while citing a strong pipeline and payoff timing dynamics. (CEO & Director Hardwick; President Stewart)

  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4’s emphasis on CDs and operating leverage to Q1’s focus on margin trajectory post-loan sale, noninterest-bearing deposit remix sustainability, and specialty vertical monetization. (Multiple analysts)

Risks and concerns

  • "We added a $12 million nonaccrual office relationship" and the largest nonaccrual relationship included "2 office-related exposures totaling roughly $12 million." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Martin)

  • "C&I line utilization increased modestly to 51%, which we view as healthy borrower activity rather than stress." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Martin)

  • On the mortgage repositioning, management tied the near-term impact to capital and earnings optics: "a $29.8 million mark-to-market charge" with "a tangible book value earn-back of approximately 4 years." (CEO & Director Hardwick)

Final takeaway

Management emphasized that Q1 results reflected the First Savings closing, a planned $357 million mortgage loan repositioning expected to be completed by the end of Q2, and continued capital return. Executives reiterated confidence in mid-single-digit 2026 loan growth, guided to a $111 million to $114 million quarterly expense run rate as synergies are realized after the May integration, and described margin as positioned for a few basis points of improvement over the year while deposit costs remain steady and the balance sheet remix continues.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Liberty Energy targets 3 gigawatts of deployed power by 2029 as it expects sequential revenue growth in Q2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Liberty Energy (LBRT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Revenue of $1 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $126 million reflected record pumping efficiencies and high fleet utilization, while absorbing the full realization of pricing headwinds and winter weather disruption," said (President, CEO & Director Ron Gusek).

  • Gusek tied the near-term completions setup to tightening capacity and customer demand, saying, "we definitely feel like the market is quite tight today from a utilization standpoint" and "we will start to recognize some of that price here in the second quarter."

  • On power, Gusek described rising behind-the-meter demand and a shift in counterparties, saying Liberty is seeing "more direct collaboration with hyperscalers" as customers increasingly prioritize "fully integrated end-to-end power solutions."

  • "During the first quarter, we executed $1.3 billion in convertible debt offerings," Gusek said, adding they entered "capped call transactions at a 150% premium to the reference share price" and reiterated a "2029 goal of reaching 3 gigawatts of deployed power."

  • "In the first quarter of '26, revenue was $1 billion," and "first quarter adjusted EBITDA was $126 million," said (CFO & Treasurer Michael Stock). Stock added, "we closed the quarter delivering record level output, generating more horsepower hours in the quarter than ever before in our 15-year operating history."

Outlook

  • Management guided to a stronger Q2 sequential setup, with Gusek stating, "In the second quarter, we expect sequential growth in revenue on increased utilization and corresponding improvements in profitability."

  • Stock provided a top-line directional marker for Q2, saying, "I'd say high single digits kind of up on the revenue side, but mostly activity like pull-throughs," and added, "normal incrementals through the EBITDA sideline for activity incrementals is probably the best way to look at it."

  • On completions pricing cadence, Gusek told analysts, "meaningfully in Q3 is the right way to think about it" for pricing impact, with "very modest impact this quarter" and a larger effect "in the back half of the year."

  • Capex stance was maintained, with Stock saying, "it really hasn't changed at the moment" and "we're still on target for about where we were on that CapEx guide," while noting Liberty may revisit in July.

Financial Results

  • Stock reported Q1 net income of $23 million, adjusted net income of $10 million, and fully diluted EPS of $0.14 (adjusted $0.06).

  • Cost and profitability items included G&A of $60 million (including "noncash stock-based compensation of approximately $6 million"), other income of $10 million (including "$17 million of gain on investments" offset by "interest expense of approximately $8 million"), and a tax expense of $9 million ("approximately 29% of pretax income").

  • Balance sheet and cash flow highlights included cash of $699 million, net debt of $579 million, total liquidity of $1.2 billion, Q1 net capex and long-term deposits of $133 million, $15 million in dividends, and $24 million of asset-sale proceeds.

  • On power funding mechanics, Stock said Liberty has "planned contract milestone payments of approximately $300 million in the second quarter or early part of the third quarter" to "secure generation capacity in support of our 3 gigawatt plan for 2029."

Q&A

  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: asked about completion activity, pricing timing, and power marketing focus; (CEO Gusek) said capacity returning is "a relatively limited amount of equipment" requiring "meaningful capital investment," and on power said, "we've definitely not chosen to focus specifically on one area," while (CFO Stock) emphasized "long-term build-own-operate contracts, 10 to 20 years in duration."

  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: asked about hyperscaler direct engagement and digiFleet additions; (CEO Gusek) said conversations are "directly with the hyperscalers," and the 2026 digiPrime builds were expected as "replacement equipment" but Liberty has "optionality" to add a fleet if the opportunity has the right "price, margin and really duration outlook."

  • Stephen Gengaro, Stifel: pressed on when pricing shows up and Vantage economics; (CEO Gusek) said pricing shows "meaningfully in Q3," while (CFO Stock) explained the 400 MW Vantage structure has a payment stream that "mirrors the equivalent of an ESA over a 5-year period" for the generation portion.

  • Joshua Silverstein, UBS: asked about Vantage milestones and international expansion; (CEO Gusek) said Liberty is already taking delivery of equipment and building a "backlog of assets," but "we just -- we don't have spare equipment" for international, while noting Australia and saying "we'll have first gas celebration there" around "maybe August."

  • Derek Podhaizer, Piper Sandler: asked about longer-term frac tightness and the canceled 330 MW campus; (CEO Gusek) said a growth scenario tightens quickly with limited sidelined fleets and build lead times "probably takes 9 months," while (CFO Stock) said the hyperscaler "decided to delay" and the developer paid "a multimillion dollar cancellation fee" and the 330 MW was reallocated.

  • Keith MacKey, RBC: asked about power pipeline acceleration and variable speed benefits; (CEO Gusek) said the pipeline is "certainly accelerating" and includes campuses "gigawatt scale or larger," while describing variable speed as enabling a path to "100% natural gas on location" and removing diesel.

  • Saurabh Pant, BofA: asked for Q2 guideposts and timing risk in power; (CFO Stock) reiterated "high single digits" revenue growth, while (CEO Gusek) said Liberty’s pipeline is "meaningfully larger" than available generation and, "we remain very, very confident in our ability to deploy that 3 gigawatts... in 2029."

  • Daniel Kutz, Morgan Stanley: asked about Q2 incrementals and converts timing; (CFO Stock) reiterated "normal incrementals" and said the converts were "opportunistic," describing "less than 3% net cost of capital" and citing macro uncertainty as a reason to secure funding.

  • Marc Bianchi, TD Cowen: asked about capex guide; (CFO Stock) said it is unchanged for now and may be revisited in July.

  • John Daniel, Daniel Energy Partners: asked whether pricing is tied to diesel; (CEO Gusek) said it is "not necessarily formulaic" and "inevitably, it ends up becoming a conversation," adding Liberty will not "recoup the entire value" of the diesel-driven opportunity.

  • Edward Kim, Barclays: asked about 3 GW order status; (CFO Stock) said "the vast majority of it is either ordered or in contractual negotiations" and "that's all going to be kind of in flight this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive but probing, with repeated focus on near-term completions pricing timing, Q2 EBITDA cadence, and power-contract timing risk, including questions on cancellation outcomes and supply/demand.

  • Management tone was positive and confident on activity tightening and power demand, repeatedly using firm language such as "we remain very, very confident" and "we expect" on Q2 improvement, while acknowledging timing variables in power projects are not fully controllable.

  • Versus last quarter, management commentary shifted from expecting Q1 weakness due to "pricing headwinds and winter weather disruption" (Q4 call) to emphasizing demand inquires accelerating and Q2 sequential improvement, while analysts maintained sustained attention on execution timelines and contract structure.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 results and commentary aligned with the prior-quarter setup that Q1 would see "the full realization of pricing headwinds and winter weather disruption" (Q4 call), but the current call added a tighter utilization narrative and earlier pricing recovery discussions.

  • Power messaging remained centered on the 3 GW by 2029 plan and long-duration contracts, but Q1 emphasized more direct hyperscaler engagement and disclosed the 330 MW expansion delay and associated cancellation fee.

  • Funding posture evolved from discussing project finance funding pathways (Q4 call) to detailing executed convertible raises and how forward payments for generation are being financed and recycled into project finance.

Risks and Concerns

  • On completions, Gusek pointed to limited restartable capacity and the need for "meaningful capital investment" to bring sidelined fleets back, while noting pricing changes require time to "work through pads" before showing up.

  • On power, management acknowledged schedule slippage risk, with Gusek stating, "There are variables that are at play on any given site that remain beyond our -- what we're focused on," and highlighted mitigation via an oversized pipeline and deeper direct work with hyperscalers.

  • The company flagged market volatility tied to geopolitical developments, and Stock said Liberty considered potential "knock-on effects" on financial markets when choosing to raise capital.

Final Takeaway

Liberty’s leadership framed Q1 as operationally strong despite pricing and weather impacts, while positioning Q2 for sequential revenue growth and normal incremental margins. Management emphasized a tightening North American completions market with pricing expected to show more meaningfully in Q3, and highlighted stepped-up power execution and contracting efforts—supported by $1.3B of convertibles and expected milestone payments—as it reiterates a target to reach 3 gigawatts of deployed power by 2029.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Xiao-I plans ADS ratio change equivalent to reverse split
data.description:
• Xiao-I Corporation (AIXI) on Thursday said it plans to change the ratio of its American Depositary Shares to ordinary shares, effectively carrying out a one-for-twenty reverse ADS split.
  • The company said the new ratio will be one ADS representing 60 ordinary shares, compared with the current one ADS representing one-third of an ordinary share.

  • Xiao-I said the change is expected to take effect on or about May 11, 2026, with ADSs continuing to trade on the Nasdaq Stock Market under the same ticker.

  • AIXI --34.88% after hours to $0.7032.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Hexcel reaffirms 2026 adjusted EPS of $2.10 to $2.30 while expecting A320 volumes at the low end of low-700s
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hexcel (HXL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Our first quarter results were in line with our expectations" as "an improving commercial market" showed "higher production levels and channel inventory levels normalizing" after 2025 destocking, according to (CEO, President & Chairman Thomas Gentile).
  • Gentile said the company is watching geopolitical developments: "we are monitoring developments closely" and "most of what we buy is under long-term contracts" while Hexcel "hedge[s] propylene... for 8 quarters" to mitigate feedstock and logistics volatility.
  • On commercial aerospace, Gentile reported "sales of $502 million" and "adjusted earnings per share were $0.59," adding that "rising commercial aerospace demand drove earnings" and "gross margins also improved compared to last year."
  • Gentile said Commercial Aerospace sales were "$334 million" and up "18.8%" year-over-year, with gains across "the Airbus A350 and A320 and the Boeing 787 and 737 MAX," and he updated program expectations: "we now expect our volumes on the A320 to be at the lower end of our guidance of low 700 for the year" while remaining "confident" in A350 "80 units in 2026, perhaps even with some upside."
  • In Defense, Space & Other, Gentile reported "sales of $169 million" were affected by "the divestment of our Austrian facility," while "just Defense & Space... increased low single digits" and missile-related demand is expected to "begin to impact us favorably later this year."
  • (Executive VP & Interim CFO Michael Lenz) said "total first quarter 2026 sales of $502 million" reflected commercial ramp, and "gross margin of 26.9%" benefited from "volume, mix and price realization" plus "a nonrecurring favorable effect from the timing of inventory utilized."

Outlook

  • Lenz stated: "we have reaffirmed our 2026 guidance, including adjusted EPS of $2.10 to $2.30," and added, "Our expectation remains for a roughly even split between the first and second half of 2026."
  • Gentile reiterated program puts and takes as offsetting: "we see basically a flat outcome for the year in terms of our plan" with "higher on A350 and 737, flat on 87 and a little down on the 320," and concluded, "we are maintaining our full year guidance."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of 2026 assumptions, the A320 outlook shifted from "low to mid-700s" (Q4 2025 call) to "the lower end" of the low-700s range (Q1 2026 call), tied to "A320 engine availability" per Gentile.

Financial Results

  • Lenz reported segment mix and profitability: Commercial Aerospace was "$333 million" (about "66%" of sales) and Defense, Space & Other was "$169 million" (about "34%"), while "adjusted operating income... was $68 million or 13.5% of sales."
  • Lenz said FX turned into a headwind: "First quarter 2026 operating margin was negatively impacted by approximately 80 basis points from foreign exchange" versus a "favorable impact of approximately 60 basis points" in Q1 2025.
  • Lenz reported cash flow improvement: "Net cash provided by operating activities... was $19 million" and "free cash flow... was a use of $6 million," noting Q1 is "historically a cash use quarter" but "less than typical" this year.
  • On leverage and capital return, Lenz reported "Leverage... was 2.6x" and said the ASR "concluded... with approximately 4.5 million shares repurchased" and "the remaining authorization... was $381 million," while the board declared an "$0.18 quarterly dividend."

Q&A

  • David Strauss, Wells Fargo: asked about commercial growth outlook and margin incrementals; CEO Gentile: "we're basically saying that we're going to hold to our guidance" and cited Q1 margin drivers including "price" on renewals and "inventory... on the books at a lower cost."
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: asked how A350 flows to margin and defense acceleration; CEO Gentile: "when our volume goes up, we get better operating leverage" and on missiles "we start to see it really jump in the third and fourth quarter."
  • Scott Mikus, Melius: asked about restocking and pricing; CEO Gentile: "we're not seeing the destocking" and pricing was "several contracts... up for renewal" with better alignment to "inflation" and costs.
  • Myles Walton, Wolfe: asked whether half/half guidance split was sales or EPS and about margin cadence; CFO Lenz: "I was referring to EPS" and noted "start-up costs" and hiring phasing as lines restart.
  • Myles Walton, Wolfe: asked about M&A; CEO Gentile: "we're not really planning on any M&A until we get" leverage "under 2 by the end of this year."
  • Kenneth Herbert, RBC: asked about Europe input-cost risk and next-gen aircraft timing; CEO Gentile: "over 90%" of inputs are regional and Hexcel has a "forward buying program"; on next-gen timing "No, it hasn't changed."
  • Gautam Khanna, TD Cowen: asked for Q1 shipment/build-rate context; CEO Gentile: "A350 was at about 7" and "On the MAX... in the 40 range."
  • Jordan Lyonnais, BofA: asked about hiring; CEO Gentile: "we expect to hire around 400 people" and "through March, we hired about 200."
  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: asked about R&D run-rate and implied EPS cadence; CFO Lenz: Q1 increase was "timing" and some "bucket shift"; CEO Gentile: "we feel it's prudent right now to just hold the line and maintain guidance."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical around sustainability of Q1 margins and EPS cadence, including questions implying conservatism such as Deutsche Bank’s view that guidance suggests "pressure to grow EPS off this first quarter base."
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing balance and prudence: "we're going to hold to our guidance" and "we feel it's prudent right now to just hold the line."
  • Versus the prior quarter’s more upbeat framing of a ramp becoming "into focus," Q1 2026 maintained confidence but added more explicit offsets (A320 engines, FX, startup costs) while still stating guidance is maintained.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 highlighted commercial recovery execution and specific program adjustments, while Q4 2025 centered on setting 2026 guidance ("adjusted EPS between $2.10 and $2.30") and describing catalysts across A350/A320/787/737.
  • The A350 narrative shifted from Q4’s "lingering destocking" to Q1’s "channel destocking largely behind us" and "increasing alignment" with Airbus build rates, while the A320 moved down within the same broad range.
  • Capital structure updates advanced from Q4’s note that leverage was "just under 2.7x" post-ASR to Q1’s refinancing of the "$750 million" revolver to "2031" and leverage at "2.6x," alongside a stated plan to return to "1.5 to 2x during 2026."

Risks and Concerns

  • Gentile flagged volatility risk from the Middle East: "some of the inputs to our products are petroleum-based" and Hexcel is focused on "taking actions to protect our business from near-term cost volatility" via contracts and hedging.
  • Lenz said FX is now a headwind "following a lag resulting from our hedging program," and management also cited potential cost spillovers if higher oil prices persist (shipping and logistics).
  • Execution risk was discussed through line restarts and staffing: Lenz referenced "start-up costs" and Gentile confirmed hiring "around 400" direct labor to support ramps.

Final Takeaway

Hexcel management described Q1 2026 as a solid start driven by commercial aerospace volume recovery, improved operating leverage, and better cash performance than typical for a first quarter, while reaffirming full-year 2026 adjusted EPS guidance of $2.10 to $2.30. Management emphasized that upside on A350 and Boeing narrow-body demand is being offset by lower expected A320 volumes tied to engine availability, and it positioned hedging, long-term supply contracts, and disciplined capacity bring-ups as key tools to navigate FX and energy-related uncertainty.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sports Entertainment Gaming Global gets Nasdaq notice over delayed filing
data.description:
• Sports Entertainment Gaming Global Corporation (SEGG) on Thursday said it received a notice from Nasdaq Stock Market on April 17, for failing to timely file its annual Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2025.
  • The company said it can submit a plan within 60 days to regain compliance, and Nasdaq may grant up to 180 days from the filing deadline, or until October 12, 2026, to meet the requirement.
  • It added that it intends to file the Form 10-K as soon as possible.
  • SEGG +1.89% after hours to $0.6844.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: ASUR outlines 98% maximum tariff objective as Motiva deal targets Q2 close
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Grupo Aeroportuario del Sureste (ASR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We expected the first quarter of 2026 to reflect a period of transition for ASUR" (CEO, Director of Finance and Chief Financial & Strategic Planning Officer Adolfo Castro Rivas), adding that results were pressured "by the security-related events beginning on February 22" and "the TSA-related disruptions in the U.S. airports, which also impacted Puerto Rico."
  • "This makes the first full quarter of consolidation of our U.S. commercial platform" (CEO Castro Rivas), and he said, "We expect gradual improvement as the platform scales, supported by the new commercial openings in Terminal 8 and the upcoming opening of the Terminal 1 this year, both at GF."
  • "We remain focused on completing the Motiva transaction, which is now pending remaining regulatory approvals and is expected to close in the second quarter this year" (CEO Castro Rivas), calling it "a step change in scale and geographic diversification" and reiterating: "Our strategy remains consistent, diversifying our revenue base, including a greater focus on nonregulated revenue."
  • "Total passenger traffic increased 1.9% year-on-year" (CEO Castro Rivas), with "Colombia... up 11%" and "Traffic in Cancun declined 2% during the quarter, while the other 8 airports in Mexico grew by 5%."
  • "Total revenues increased 2.2% year-on-year, reaching MXN 8.4 billion" (CEO Castro Rivas), and he attributed this to "a nearly 9% increase in non-aeronautical revenues" including "approximately MXN 438 million in non-aeronautical revenue" from the U.S. platform.

Outlook

  • "We believe these factors were temporary and do not reflect a change in the underlying demand" (CEO Castro Rivas) regarding the security- and TSA-related disruptions.
  • "As we move through the year, we expect to see difficult operating conditions, including higher fuel prices and recent capacity reductions" (CEO Castro Rivas).
  • "We... expected to close in the second quarter this year" (CEO Castro Rivas) on Motiva, while emphasizing: "We intend to fund the acquisition with debt" was not reiterated on this call; the current call focused on regulatory timing and portfolio diversification.
  • "The objective we have this year is 98%" (CEO Castro Rivas) on maximum tariff, adding, "for the moment, we do not see why we should not be reaching the 98%."

Financial Results

  • "Total revenues increased 2.2% year-on-year, reaching MXN 8.4 billion" (CEO Castro Rivas), while "aeronautical revenues declined low single digits" and "commercial revenues increased nearly 7%."
  • "Total expenses increased 25% year-on-year" (CEO Castro Rivas), citing U.S. integration, Colombia depreciation/amortization dynamics, and fees; he added, "MXN 91 million is what we have paid as a professional fees to get the U.S. acquisition" and noted U.S. one-time items "of approximately MXN 70 million more."
  • "Consolidated EBITDA decreased nearly 6%" and "The adjusted EBITDA margin declined nearly 600 basis points to 64.1%" (CEO Castro Rivas), driven mainly by "the ramp-up of the U.S. operations" and amortization changes in Colombia.
  • "Net majority income declined 20% year-on-year to MXN 2.8 billion" (CEO Castro Rivas), citing "higher depreciation and amortization" and "increased interest expenses following the recent financings."
  • "We closed the quarter with cash of MXN 13.8 billion and net debt to EBITDA of 0.8x" (CEO Castro Rivas), and "Capital expenditures totaled MXN 544 million" with "CapEx for the full year... totaled MXN 7.9 billion."

Q&A

  • Rodolfo Ramos, Bradesco BBI: asked about U.S. commercial seasonality/12-month EBITDA and Motiva closing costs, and whether World Cup could pressure Cancun; CEO Castro Rivas: "the current operation we have is not exactly what we will have... at the end of this year" and said, "the EVA this year should be close to, let's say, MXN 20 million," while adding on World Cup, "I don't think so."
  • Guilherme Mendes, JPMorgan: asked if Mexico traffic could turn positive and what commercial upside/synergies Motiva brings; CEO Castro Rivas: "Everything went well up to February 22 and then basically collapsed" and added, "it is not an easy moment to be saying how the year is going to end up," while on Motiva: "I don't see synergy as an important piece of this."
  • Ernst Mortenkotter, GBM: asked if Colombia’s COP 165 billion intervention affects concession economics and about maximum tariff catch-up; CEO Castro Rivas: "we signed an amendment" allowing investment "COP 165 billion" and said it would move the phaseout timing, while reiterating the 98% tariff objective.
  • Alberto Valerio, UBS: asked how FX affects maximum tariff mechanics and whether lease/professional-fee expenses were nonrecurring; CEO Castro Rivas: "It's one basket and it's peso denominated" and confirmed the "MXN 91 million" fees and "approximately MXN 70 million" one-time U.S. items.
  • Gabriel Himelfarb Mustri, Scotiabank: asked about airline capacity shifts and what is driving commercial trends; CEO Castro Rivas: "Spirit has decreased its capacity" and on Puerto Rico disruptions said "the TSA lines... reached 4.5 hours" and linked commercial pressure to FX, noting "today MXN 17 something."
  • Andres Cardona, Citi: asked again on U.S. EBITDA contribution; CEO Castro Rivas: "this year so far, $30 million in EBITDA."
  • Francisco Suarez, Scotiabank: asked about U.S. occupancy/lease terms and rent upside; CEO Castro Rivas: "we have more than 400 contracts" and "we have all or almost all the spaces contracted," adding average lease life "between 15 to 17 years" and answering "Absolutely" on tenant improvements linking to better rents.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, pressing on U.S. EBITDA normalization and Mexico traffic visibility, with repeated questions on "how much EBITDA contribution" (Andres Cardona, Citi) and traffic outlook.
  • Management: neutral to slightly negative on near-term demand visibility, with cautionary phrasing like "we don't know how much" and "it is not an easy moment," while remaining constructive on integration and Motiva timing.
  • Versus last quarter: Q1 included more event-driven disruption detail ("security-related events" and "TSA-related") and more uncertainty language, while Q4 emphasized an "important inflection point" and expansion milestones.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 introduced a management change: "David Barlow has assumed the responsibility for Investor Relations" (CEO Castro Rivas), which was not discussed in Q4.
  • Motiva timing shifted from Q4’s "expected in the first half of 2026" to Q1’s "expected to close in the second quarter this year" (CEO Castro Rivas).
  • The U.S. asset moved from partial consolidation in Q4 to "the first full quarter of consolidation" in Q1 (CEO Castro Rivas), with Q1 also disclosing negative EBITDA for the quarter and repeated investor questions on normalized run-rate.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Security-related events" and "TSA-related screening disruptions" were cited as key traffic disruptors (CEO Castro Rivas), alongside "higher fuel prices" and "capacity reductions" as near-term operating headwinds.
  • One-time and integration-related costs were flagged, including "MXN 91 million" in professional fees and U.S. prior-year items "approximately MXN 70 million" (CEO Castro Rivas).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a transition period shaped by temporary shocks to traffic, while emphasizing a strategic pivot toward more nonregulated, dollar-linked earnings through the first full quarter of U.S. commercial consolidation and an expected Q2 close for the Motiva transaction. Alongside near-term caution on fuel prices and airline capacity, management highlighted a 98% maximum tariff objective, continued network commercial buildout, and planned 2026 openings and expansions intended to support future commercial revenue scaling.

data.title: Horizon Bancorp forecasts 2026 FTE net interest margin of 4.25%-4.35% as deposit-led growth strategy holds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Horizon Bancorp (HBNC) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Horizon's first quarter results demonstrate the core strength of our community banking model" and management highlighted profitability and balance-sheet metrics including "an annualized return on average assets above 1.60%, return on average tangible common equity above 19% and continued durability in our net interest margin at 4.29%" (CEO, President & Director Thomas Prame).

  • Management framed Q1 as deposit-led and pipeline-supported, citing "over $147 million in growth or 11% annualized" in deposits and "$34 million in growth" in commercial lending, alongside the decision that it "elected not to chase lower-yielding mortgages onto the balance sheet" during early-Q1 refinancing (CEO Prame).

  • Commercial and credit commentary emphasized mix and stability: total loans held for investment "ended the quarter at $4.87 billion" with a "$34.2 million increase in commercial loans" and residential/consumer down "$32 million"; credit included substandard loans of "$63.4 million" ("1.3% of total loans"), nonperforming loans of "$37 million" ("0.76%"), and net charge-offs of "$626,000" ("5 basis points annualized") (Executive VP & Chief Commercial Banking Officer Lynn Kerber).

  • The CFO tied margin durability to guidance despite a change in rate assumptions: "our net interest margin and net interest income outlook is unchanged from our original guidance despite going from the assumption of 2 rate cuts previously to none today" (CFO & Executive VP John Stewart).

Outlook

  • 2026 guidance was reiterated: "Period-end loan and deposit balances are still expected to grow mid-single digits"; "Non-FTE net interest income is still expected to grow in the low teens year-over-year with the FTE net interest margin in the range of 4.25% to 4.35%"; "Fee income is still expected to be in the mid-$40 million range"; "Expenses in the mid-$160 million range"; and an "effective tax rate" of "18% to 20%" (CFO Stewart).

  • Management explicitly said the removal of two previously assumed 25 bp cuts did not change the framework: "This outlook previously included the assumption for two 25 basis point rate cuts... which have now been removed. This change in assumption did not impact the outlook" (CFO Stewart).

  • For near-term expense cadence, management flagged a Q2 seasonal step-up: "we would anticipate a modest increase in quarterly expense run rate in Q2 related to the full impact of annual merit increases and planned marketing spend" while keeping the full-year view unchanged (CFO Stewart).

Financial results

  • Margin and balance-sheet dynamics were described as steady, with Q1 net interest margin "unchanged from the prior quarter at 4.29%" and Q1 average interest-earning cash balances exceeding internal projections "by about $60 million," which "negatively impact[ed] the margin percentage by about 4 basis points" (CFO Stewart).

  • The CFO provided asset-yield context: "New loan production in the quarter exceeded 6.6% compared with average loan yields in the quarter of 6.28% and roll-off yields just below 6%"; the investment portfolio was expected to see "$75 million to $100 million of principal cash flows over the balance of the year at about 4.7%" with "Reinvestment rates in Q1" of "4.8%" (CFO Stewart).

  • Noninterest income and expense items were framed around underlying trends and the expense base: "fees were up about 13% year-over-year" (excluding items the CFO specified) and quarterly expenses were "$40.7 million" with "no change" to the "mid-$160 million" full-year expense outlook (CFO Stewart).

  • Capital generation remained a centerpiece: "capital ratios improved quite strongly" with "CET1 up 40 basis points to 10.82%" and management said it was driven by "strong profitability levels" and "a modest sequential decline in risk-weighted assets" (CFO Stewart).

Q&A

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: asked about deposit-driven excess cash and near-term margin trajectory; CFO Stewart said extra cash "didn't impact net interest income" but had "a modest impact" on NIM, and management expected to be "a modest net user of cash" in Q2 while being comfortable if cash stayed "between 2% and 3%" of earning assets.

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: asked if any part of the outlook felt better or worse versus three months ago; CEO Prame answered: "I go with... as expected, the outlook looks very similar."

  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: asked whether deposit wins came from M&A disruption; CEO Prame said it was "a true step-up in our organic efforts" and "not a strategy targeted at one specific institution."

  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: asked if commercial loan growth slowdown reflected payoffs; EVP Kerber said payoffs were "very consistent with our long-term averages" and attributed trends to "seasonality" and being "selective in where we're lending."

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked about equipment finance build-out; EVP Kerber said the unit was "effectively year 2" and running "between our year 2 and year 3 of the plan" and "it's been going really well."

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked what could drive upside to guidance; CEO Prame pointed to "accelerating our deposits" and potential "talent adds" in markets including "Grand Rapids, Lansing, Detroit... Indianapolis."

  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: pressed on capital deployment given strong capital build; CEO Prame said optionality includes "buyback of shares" and other reinvestment, adding: "It's really not going to burn a hole in our pocket."

  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: asked what drove optimism in commercial loan outlook and whether teams could be added amid disruption; EVP Kerber said the bank was "sticking to our knitting" with no major model changes and that while they had capacity, they "always have an eye for talent" and would act "opportunist[ically]."

  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: asked about yield pickup and growth appetite in consumer/residential; CFO Stewart reiterated new production "just above 6.6%" vs roll-off dynamics, and CEO Prame said mortgage/consumer weakness was "episodic" and that management did not pursue "pricing sub-6%" fixed-rate assets; he added mortgage/consumer could be "relatively flat, maybe mildly up, mildly down."

  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: asked about M&A posture and payback expectations; CEO Prame said there was "not a specific plan right now" to pursue M&A, and CFO Stewart said the bank may accept longer paybacks for "stock repurchases" than for acquisitions, noting acquisitions carry "execution risk" and "integration risk."

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts): multiple questions probed sustainability and sensitivity—cash drag on NIM, whether guidance could improve, capital deployment, M&A appetite, and whether market disruption is creating opportunities.

  • Slightly positive (management): prepared remarks and Q&A repeatedly emphasized steadiness and discipline, including "we feel good" about Q1 performance (CFO Stewart) and "as expected" guidance (CEO Prame), while acknowledging tactical headwinds like cash levels and episodic mortgage pricing.

  • Compared with Q4 2025: management maintained a similar confident tone around a "durable" and rate-neutral balance sheet, but Q1 introduced a clearer pivot from assumed rate cuts to "none today" without changing guidance (CFO Stewart), while analyst focus stayed concentrated on deposit-led growth, margin drivers, and capital optionality.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent quarter-to-quarter (mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth; FTE net interest margin 4.25%-4.35%; fee income mid-$40 million; expenses mid-$160 million; tax rate 18%-20%), but Q1 removed the prior assumption of two rate cuts while keeping the targets intact (CFO Stewart).

  • Strategic emphasis shifted from Q4’s balance-sheet repositioning completion to Q1’s deployment of strong deposit inflows and reinforcing pricing discipline—Q1 described elevated cash as a NIM headwind and highlighted the decision not to add "lower-yielding mortgages" (CEO Prame; CFO Stewart).

  • Analyst questioning in Q1 leaned more into capital deployment tools (buybacks vs. M&A) and monetizing market disruption, while Q4 questioning more heavily unpacked guidance levers and normalization of credit metrics.

Risks and concerns

  • Competitive lending and rate conditions remained a backdrop: "a competitive lending landscape and a dynamic rate environment" (EVP Kerber) and management acknowledged mortgage pricing that it "elected not to play" when longer-duration fixed-rate yields were "sub-6%" (CEO Prame).

  • Credit risk was framed as manageable but with modestly higher nonperformers: EVP Kerber said nonperforming loans "have increased modestly over recent quarters" but are "manageable," and added that certain credits were expected to return to performing status, pay off, or complete collections with loans "well secured and/or appropriately reserved."

  • Execution risk centered on funding and deployment choices: CFO Stewart noted interest-bearing deposit costs could be "flat to up" with no cuts, while management emphasized that balance-sheet growth will be "driven by deposit growth" and that capital deployment decisions would remain "very disciplined" (CFO Stewart; CEO Prame).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a strong start built on durable profitability and capital generation, with deposit growth outpacing expectations and commercial pipelines supporting mid-single-digit balance-sheet growth targets. Horizon reiterated its full-year framework—FTE net interest margin of 4.25% to 4.35%, low-teens non-FTE net interest income growth, fee income in the mid-$40 million range, and expenses in the mid-$160 million range—while stating that removing assumed rate cuts did not change the outlook. In Q&A, analysts focused on cash deployment and margin mechanics, capital optionality (including buybacks and M&A), and competitive dynamics, while management repeatedly emphasized pricing discipline, deposit-led growth, and a measured approach to deploying rising capital levels.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Digital Realty Trust Q1 sees $1.64B revenue beat; backlog hits $1.8B
Strategic Education expects Q1 to be 2026 low point as it targets 200 bps margin expansion via faster AI productivity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Strategic Education, Inc. (STRA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our first quarter results reflect meaningful progress across 3 of our primary strategic objectives: the continued investment and growth of our Education Technology Services division; growing our employer-focused strategy; and further implementing our AI and other productivity enabling systems.” (President, CEO & Director Karl McDonnell)

  • “For the first quarter, SEI revenue declined 1% year-over-year, driven by a slight decrease in consolidated enrollment.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) McDonnell also said, “Based on our current enrollment trends, we expect that the first quarter will be the low point of the year in both absolute revenue and revenue growth.”

  • “Our productivity initiatives drove a 2% reduction in adjusted operating expenses, resulting in 3% operating income growth and slight margin expansion to 14.3%.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) He added, “Adjusted earnings per share came in at $1.41.”

  • “Education Technology Services grew revenue 21% to $42 million.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) He said ETS operating income “grew 42% to $20 million and a 47% margin,” and that “ETS now represents 46% of consolidated operating income.”

  • “Workforce Edge ended the quarter with 82 corporate agreements, covering 4 million employees, and enrollments from Workforce Edge into either Strayer or Capella University grew 70%, reaching nearly 4,000 students.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

  • “Employer-affiliated enrollment grew 10% and reached a new all-time high of 34.5% of total U.S. Higher Education enrollment.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) He also said U.S. Higher Education “delivered $26 million of operating income and a 12% margin,” and “set a new record for average student retention at 89%.”

  • “We repurchased approximately 493,000 shares during the quarter, for a total of $40 million.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) He added, “we have approximately $200 million remaining on our share repurchase authorization through the end of the year.”

  • “The Capella margin, probably not surprising, is much higher than Strayer and is driving most of the operating income for U.S. Higher Ed.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Daniel Jackson)

Outlook

  • No formal EPS or revenue guidance was provided in the Q1 2026 transcript; management framed expectations through enrollment trends, productivity, and the “notional plan.”

  • “I have very high confidence that we're going to be on our notional plan this year.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) He clarified, “when I'm talking about our performance against the notional plan, I'm predominantly referring to EBIT and EPS.”

  • “The AI and other technological enablements of productivity are being implemented a little faster than even I expected.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) He said this underpinned his expectation “that we're going to get to the 200 basis points of margin expansion.”

  • On U.S. Higher Education enrollment, “I think it'd be very close. I think we have a good chance to do that.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) On ANZ, “I'm hopeful… that we're going to have full year new student growth… Whether or not we get to total enrollment growth, it will depend.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

Financial Results

  • “SEI revenue declined 1% year-over-year.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell) He attributed the decline to “a slight decrease in consolidated enrollment.”

  • “Adjusted operating expenses” were down 2%, while “operating income” grew 3% and “margin” expanded to 14.3%. (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

  • Segment profitability mix shifted further toward ETS as “ETS now represents 46% of consolidated operating income.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

  • U.S. Higher Education saw revenue down 4% as “somewhat higher discounts and scholarships… together lowered revenue per student,” while “operating expenses” were down 2%. (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

  • ANZ performance reflected regulatory limits and seasonality as McDonnell said, “We reported an operating loss of $2.4 million for the quarter… [reflecting] the normal seasonality of that business.”

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: “Any idea… when we might see” the “notional plan”? President, CEO & Director McDonnell: “enrollment trends in U.S. Higher Education have been improving,” and “I have very high confidence that we're going to be on our notional plan this year… from an EBIT and EPS standpoint.”

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: asked about July 1 grad/pro loan caps under the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act.” President, CEO & Director McDonnell: “I've not heard of any demand-related issues… and… I don't expect that we're going to have a major impact.”

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: pressed on the notional plan (4% to 6% revenue growth and 200 bps margin improvement) and whether cost actions could offset lower revenue. President, CEO & Director McDonnell: “we control our expenses,” citing faster AI-driven productivity and saying, “that gives me high confidence that we're going to get to the 200 basis points of margin expansion.”

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: asked what drove sequential improvement in unaffiliated enrollment declines and whether marketing changed. President, CEO & Director McDonnell: “Capella's new student enrollment [is] quite strong,” while Strayer unaffiliated undergrad weakness “has been improving,” and marketing has shifted as “Strayer is probably down by 50% or more and Capella is up by 50% or more.”

  • Jasper Bibb, Truist Securities: asked about Capella vs. Strayer margins and Strayer fixed-cost rightsizing. Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Jackson: Strayer has “a positive margin,” with “opportunities… [in] productivity… and continued real estate rationalization,” but “it's unlikely to get to where Capella is.”

  • Jasper Bibb, Truist Securities: asked about revenue per student stability after Q1 decline. Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Jackson: “we're expecting relatively stable revenue per student for the full year,” and “with pricing that takes effect starting in the second quarter… full year revenue per student is still likely to be flat.”

  • Jasper Bibb, Truist Securities: asked whether Workforce Edge growth was slowing and what to expect in 2026. President, CEO & Director McDonnell: Sophia “should be able to continue to support 20-plus percent growth,” Workforce Edge may face anniversary effects, but “we have a very robust pipeline… [and] continue to get unsolicited inbound RFPs every quarter.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on visibility into the notional plan, regulatory funding risk, and the pace of U.S. unaffiliated enrollment declines (e.g., “the declines seem to be getting a bit worse” in the prior-quarter framing, and “what explains the sequential improvement?” in the current quarter). (Analysts)

  • Slightly positive management tone anchored on confidence and execution pace, with repeated conviction on margin expansion and productivity (e.g., “very high confidence,” and AI is being implemented “a little faster than even I expected”). (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management’s messaging shifted from celebrating large reported expansion to emphasizing Q1 as a trough and leaning more heavily on expense control to support the notional plan if revenue is softer (including “a little less revenue… [and] better expense management”).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The narrative moved from Q4’s “very pleased” results and broad-based margin expansion to Q1’s acknowledgement of topline pressure, with McDonnell saying Q1 should be “the low point of the year” for revenue and growth.

  • The notional model remained the anchor, but the path shifted: in Q4, McDonnell answered “Yes” that the notional model was “a good proxy for 2026”; in Q1, he emphasized achieving the plan “predominantly… [via] EBIT and EPS” and highlighted accelerated AI productivity as a larger driver.

  • ETS continued to be positioned as a key engine; Q1 highlighted ETS as 46% of consolidated operating income and detailed Workforce Edge student flows into Strayer/Capella, while Q4 emphasized ETS’s record year and growing share of operating income.

  • ANZ outlook became more uncertain in Q1 due to unexpected visa approval friction “even when you're below your cap,” a factor not raised in Q4’s expectation of turning to growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Regulatory and funding uncertainty risk was addressed as McDonnell said the company was “still waiting on final language” on grad loan limits, but he added he did not expect “a major impact.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

  • ANZ regulatory execution risk increased as McDonnell flagged “more friction” in visa approvals, which “may impact our ability to generate total enrollment growth this year.” (President, CEO & Director McDonnell)

  • Mix and pricing risk in U.S. Higher Education was tied to “higher discounts and scholarships” and mix-driven variability; Jackson said these drivers are “hard to predict.” (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Administrative Officer Jackson)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a revenue trough while reiterating confidence in delivering the company’s notional plan through accelerated AI-driven productivity and cost control, even if revenue growth is softer. ETS was positioned as an increasingly important profit driver, with Sophia subscription growth and Workforce Edge’s expanding corporate network feeding both services revenue and student enrollments into Strayer and Capella. In U.S. Higher Education, the call underscored a continued mix shift toward employer-affiliated and health care enrollment alongside stable full-year revenue-per-student expectations, while ANZ faced added uncertainty from slower visa approvals despite domestic new-student momentum and efforts to maximize international enrollment within caps.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Mobileye targets $1.975B 2026 revenue midpoint as EyeQ demand stays strong and share buyback launches
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We delivered very good results in the first quarter. Revenue was up 27% year-over-year. Adjusted operating income was up 61% and our operating cash flow was again strong at $75 million" (Co-Founder, CEO, President & Director Amnon Shashua).
  • "As a result of higher volume and revenue in Q1, we have raised our 2026 outlook towards the high end of our original guidance" (CEO Shashua), while adding, "The geopolitical and economic environment remains volatile, but based on our visibility for Q2, we believe there is sufficient conservatism baked into the second half" (CEO Shashua).
  • "We have seen upward pressure on demand for our EyeQ product for the last several quarters. That continued in Q1 and is what we expect for Q2 as well" (CEO Shashua), and "India looks like a meaningful growth opportunity" (CEO Shashua).
  • "On the buyback we announced this morning, we are a cash-generative company, which is unique in this space" (CEO Shashua), and "we see an opportunity to deploy cash towards share repurchase" (CEO Shashua).
  • "First quarter revenue of $558 million was up 27% year-over-year" (Chief Financial Officer Moran Rojansky), and "we also excluded the goodwill impairment loss referenced in the press release and transaction costs associated with the Mentee acquisition, which closed in early February" (CFO Rojansky).

Outlook

  • "We are increasing the revenue outlook to $1.975 billion at the midpoint" (CFO Rojansky), and "we are increasing our outlook for adjusted operating income to $210 million at the midpoint, up from $195 million in the prior outlook" (CFO Rojansky).
  • "Turning up to second quarter. We are assuming about 9.3 million EyeQ units and for revenue to decrease approximately 6% on a year-over-year basis" (CFO Rojansky).
  • "It also assumes that the run rate of China OEM volume in the second half of 2026 comes down meaningfully from the first half levels. We aren't sure what will happen, but given low visibility on that part of the business, we prefer to stay conservative" (CFO Rojansky).

Financial results

  • "Adjusted operating income was $95 million" and "Adjusted operating margin was 17%" (CFO Rojansky).
  • "Operating expenses were as expected, representing about 25% of our full year expectation of around $1.1 billion" (CFO Rojansky).
  • "We recognized a goodwill impairment of $3.8 billion" (CFO Rojansky), adding, "on the business aspect, we kept the same projections, but reflected a higher risk premium because of macroeconomic environment, geopolitical environment" (CFO Rojansky).

Q&A

  • Xin Yu, Deutsche Bank: asked what conditions Mobileye is seeing in ADAS channels; Chief Financial Officer Rojansky said, "we are reaffirming our guidance from January call and adjusting it for the upside that we're seeing in Q1," and attributed upside to "very strong demand" in China export volumes, rising ADAS fitment rates, and customer safety stock moving to "approximately 4, 5 weeks" (CFO Rojansky).
  • Xin Yu, Deutsche Bank: asked for a Mentee update and external proof-of-concept timing; Co-Founder, CEO Shashua said, "we're still analyzing the domains" and highlighted ongoing hardware and software iterations, including "version 4, which is the hardware to go into mass production should be ready by end of this year, early next year" (CEO Shashua).
  • Chris McNally, Evercore ISI: asked what remains to achieve “Driver Out”; CEO Shashua said, "our milestones for the Driver Out is first to start validation on the final Level 4 vehicle," and set a scaling goal: "We want to see 2027, at least 6 cities, hundreds of vehicles at minimum" (CEO Shashua).
  • Joseph Spak, UBS: asked about guidance flow-through and who signs off on moving phases for robotaxi; CFO Rojansky said the China export impact extends into Q2, and CEO Shashua added, "the driver out eventually depends on the customer, which is MOIA and Volkswagen" and "our KPIs and milestones of both parties are targeting end of 2026" (CEO Shashua).
  • George Gianarikas, Canaccord: asked about NVIDIA traction; CEO Shashua said, "At the end of the day, it's a combination of performance and cost" and described NVIDIA’s Alpamayo as "not anywhere close to production worthy" (CEO Shashua).

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing, with repeated modeling and validation questions on China mix/ASP, driver-out governance, memory supply, and competitive positioning (e.g., "what your pitch is to OEMs" on NVIDIA and requests to quantify costs).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks ("very good results" and buyback rationale), while more guarded in Q&A on disclosure limits ("We are not sharing our MTBF goals" and "we're still analyzing the domains" from CEO Shashua).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management added a more explicit stock-price framing for the buyback ("we believe that this lack of visibility has weighed on our stock price" (CEO Shashua)), while analyst scrutiny stayed centered on competitive noise, program timing, and supply-chain/mix dynamics.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 introduced a new capital allocation action: "on the buyback we announced this morning" (CEO Shashua), which was not discussed in Q4 2025.
  • Q1 2026 raised full-year targets ("increasing the revenue outlook to $1.975 billion" and adjusted operating income midpoint to "$210 million" (CFO Rojansky)), while Q4 2025 guidance framed 2026 as "flattish to 5% growth" with "around $1.1 billion" OpEx (CFO Rojansky).
  • Analyst focus broadened from Q4’s planning and program timing (Porsche/Audi SOP shifts) to Q1’s goodwill impairment, buyback rationale, and sharper interrogation of China export contribution and ASP mechanics.

Risks and concerns

  • "The geopolitical and economic environment remains volatile" (CEO Shashua), and "we prefer to stay conservative" on second-half China export volumes due to "low visibility" (CFO Rojansky).
  • "we do have some incremental costs for the ECU, particularly related to memory" (CFO Rojansky), though she added, "it's a very -- it's not something that is expected to impact significantly" and described the impact as "a few millions" (CFO Rojansky).

Final takeaway

Management emphasized stronger-than-expected Q1 demand for EyeQ, lifted 2026 revenue and adjusted operating income targets, and launched a share repurchase program that CEO Amnon Shashua tied to offsetting dilution and addressing what he called limited external visibility. Leadership positioned ADAS as a high-margin cash generator, highlighted China OEM export volumes and rising fitment rates as key drivers, and reiterated that the next major autonomy milestones remain validation and “Driver Out” progress with MOIA/Volkswagen, alongside continued execution on SuperVision and Surround ADAS programs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
